questions.stem: During the internal examination shown here, the body cavity is opened using an incision named for its shape. What is this standard autopsy incision called?

explanation.summary: The autopsy internal exam classically begins with a Y-shaped incision extending from each shoulder to the mid-chest and down the midline, allowing the thoracic and abdominal cavities to be opened as shown.
explanation.whyCorrect: The autopsy internal exam classically begins with a Y-shaped incision extending from each shoulder to the mid-chest and down the midline, allowing the thoracic and abdominal cavities to be opened as shown.

questions.stem: This gross cross-section shows a diffusely nodular, firm organ from a patient with long-standing alcohol use. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.summary: The uniform micro/macronodular architecture with intervening fibrous bands in a liver from a chronic alcohol user is characteristic of cirrhosis, the end-stage of diffuse hepatic fibrosis and regenerative nodule formation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The uniform micro/macronodular architecture with intervening fibrous bands in a liver from a chronic alcohol user is characteristic of cirrhosis, the end-stage of diffuse hepatic fibrosis and regenerative nodule formation.

questions.stem: Comparing the middle vessel panel with the bottom vessel panel, the protein-rich, cell-containing fluid leaking from the middle vessel is best explained by which process?

explanation.summary: The middle panel depicts an exudate: inflammatory mediators cause vasodilation and increased interendothelial permeability, allowing protein and cells to escape. The bottom panel shows a transudate, a low-protein ultrafiltrate driven by hydrostatic/oncotic imbalance without increased permeability.
explanation.whyCorrect: The middle panel depicts an exudate: inflammatory mediators cause vasodilation and increased interendothelial permeability, allowing protein and cells to escape. The bottom panel shows a transudate, a low-protein ultrafiltrate driven by hydrostatic/oncotic imbalance without increased permeability.

questions.stem: This cross-section of a coronary artery shows a dark occlusive mass filling the lumen. What is the most likely acute clinical consequence?

explanation.summary: An occlusive thrombus in a coronary artery interrupts blood flow to the myocardium, producing ischemic necrosis (myocardial infarction).
explanation.whyCorrect: An occlusive thrombus in a coronary artery interrupts blood flow to the myocardium, producing ischemic necrosis (myocardial infarction).

questions.stem: This gross specimen shows a large intravascular mass straddling the branch point of a pulmonary artery. What is the most immediate life-threatening consequence?

explanation.summary: A large embolus obstructing a major pulmonary artery abruptly increases right ventricular afterload, which can precipitate acute right heart failure and sudden death.
explanation.whyCorrect: A large embolus obstructing a major pulmonary artery abruptly increases right ventricular afterload, which can precipitate acute right heart failure and sudden death.

questions.stem: Panel A shows a hemorrhagic (red) infarct and Panel B a pale (white) infarct. Which tissue feature explains the red appearance seen in Panel A?

explanation.summary: Red infarcts occur in tissues with dual or collateral circulation (such as lung), where blood from the unobstructed supply seeps into the necrotic zone; pale infarcts occur in end-arterial organs like spleen.
explanation.whyCorrect: Red infarcts occur in tissues with dual or collateral circulation (such as lung), where blood from the unobstructed supply seeps into the necrotic zone; pale infarcts occur in end-arterial organs like spleen.

questions.stem: This four-panel image shows a tan-white membrane seen on endoscopy of the colon, a brown residue after its removal, and histology in which neutrophils appear to "erupt" from a crypt like a volcano. Infection by which organism most classically produces this picture?

explanation.summary: The tan-white pseudomembrane of inflammatory cells and debris, together with the crypt "volcano" eruption of neutrophils, is the hallmark of pseudomembranous colitis caused by toxin-producing Clostridioides difficile, typically after antibiotic disruption of gut flora.
explanation.whyCorrect: The tan-white pseudomembrane of inflammatory cells and debris, together with the crypt "volcano" eruption of neutrophils, is the hallmark of pseudomembranous colitis caused by toxin-producing Clostridioides difficile, typically after antibiotic disruption of gut flora.

questions.stem: These gross cardiac specimens show friable, bulky vegetations attached to the leaflets of a mitral valve and a bicuspid aortic valve. What is the underlying pathologic process?

explanation.summary: Friable, destructive valvular vegetations composed of organisms, fibrin, and inflammatory cells indicate infective endocarditis; Streptococcus viridans classically seeds abnormal or damaged valves while Staphylococcus aureus can attack even previously normal or congenitally bicuspid valves.
explanation.whyCorrect: Friable, destructive valvular vegetations composed of organisms, fibrin, and inflammatory cells indicate infective endocarditis; Streptococcus viridans classically seeds abnormal or damaged valves while Staphylococcus aureus can attack even previously normal or congenitally bicuspid valves.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman with joint pain and oral ulcers develops the facial eruption shown. This rash is most characteristic of which systemic autoimmune disease?

explanation.summary: The photosensitive butterfly (malar) rash sparing the nasolabial folds is a classic mucocutaneous manifestation of SLE, a type III hypersensitivity disease driven by immune-complex deposition.
explanation.whyCorrect: The photosensitive butterfly (malar) rash sparing the nasolabial folds is a classic mucocutaneous manifestation of SLE, a type III hypersensitivity disease driven by immune-complex deposition.

questions.stem: These panels show multinucleated giant cells with vague granulomatous inflammation and an inflamed vessel wall in a patient with sinus disease, lung nodules, and hematuria. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.summary: The triad of upper-respiratory disease, necrotizing granulomatous lung inflammation, and glomerulonephritis with PR3-ANCA defines granulomatosis with polyangiitis; histology shows giant cells, geographic granulomatous necrosis, and small-vessel vasculitis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The triad of upper-respiratory disease, necrotizing granulomatous lung inflammation, and glomerulonephritis with PR3-ANCA defines granulomatosis with polyangiitis; histology shows giant cells, geographic granulomatous necrosis, and small-vessel vasculitis.

questions.stem: The left Jones-stained panel of this HSP kidney shows a cellular proliferation filling Bowman's space and adhering to the capsule. What does this finding indicate?

explanation.summary: A cellular crescent within Bowman's space signifies severe glomerular injury with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis; the accompanying immunofluorescence shows mesangial IgA deposition typical of HSP nephritis.
explanation.whyCorrect: A cellular crescent within Bowman's space signifies severe glomerular injury with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis; the accompanying immunofluorescence shows mesangial IgA deposition typical of HSP nephritis.

questions.stem: These panels show gross irregular nodules along a thickened mitral valve leaflet with corresponding microscopic nodules of lymphocytes and macrophages. What underlying disease process do these findings represent?

explanation.summary: Small verrucae along the leaflet line of closure plus Aschoff bodies (nodules of lymphocytes and macrophages) are hallmarks of rheumatic heart disease, an autoimmune sequela of group A streptococcal infection that can progress to fibrosis and mitral stenosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Small verrucae along the leaflet line of closure plus Aschoff bodies (nodules of lymphocytes and macrophages) are hallmarks of rheumatic heart disease, an autoimmune sequela of group A streptococcal infection that can progress to fibrosis and mitral stenosis.

questions.stem: This skin finding shows red, serpiginous (snake-like) lesions with central clearing appearing during acute rheumatic fever. What is the presumed mechanism producing it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct streptococcal invasion of the dermis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Immune complex deposition in dermal vessels

explanation.summary: Erythema marginatum is a nonpruritic, migratory serpiginous rash of acute rheumatic fever attributed to antibody cross-reaction (molecular mimicry) against keratin, not to direct infection of the skin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Erythema marginatum is a nonpruritic, migratory serpiginous rash of acute rheumatic fever attributed to antibody cross-reaction (molecular mimicry) against keratin, not to direct infection of the skin.

questions.stem: These renal panels compare a normal glomerulus with a hypercellular one, an electron micrograph showing a subepithelial deposit, and immunofluorescence with coarse granular staining. What is the immunologic mechanism of this injury?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Podocyte foot process effacement without deposits

explanation.summary: Poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis shows a proliferative, hypercellular glomerulus with subepithelial 'humps' on EM and coarse granular ('lumpy-bumpy') immunofluorescence for IgG and C3, indicating immune complex-mediated injury.
explanation.whyCorrect: Poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis shows a proliferative, hypercellular glomerulus with subepithelial 'humps' on EM and coarse granular ('lumpy-bumpy') immunofluorescence for IgG and C3, indicating immune complex-mediated injury.

questions.stem: A neonate presents with bilateral eyelid swelling and mucoid ocular discharge acquired during passage through the birth canal. Which pair of organisms most classically causes this?

explanation.summary: Neonatal conjunctivitis from vertical transmission through the birth canal is classically caused by C. trachomatis and N. gonorrhoeae, making maternal screening important for prevention.
explanation.whyCorrect: Neonatal conjunctivitis from vertical transmission through the birth canal is classically caused by C. trachomatis and N. gonorrhoeae, making maternal screening important for prevention.

questions.stem: This histology field shows a nodular collection of epithelioid histiocytes and multinucleated giant cells surrounding a central zone of amorphous necrotic debris. What pattern of inflammation is depicted?

explanation.summary: Central necrosis within a granuloma of epithelioid histiocytes and multinucleated giant cells indicates caseating granulomatous inflammation, seen here in a fungal (Histoplasma) infection and typical of many mycobacterial/fungal infections.
explanation.whyCorrect: Central necrosis within a granuloma of epithelioid histiocytes and multinucleated giant cells indicates caseating granulomatous inflammation, seen here in a fungal (Histoplasma) infection and typical of many mycobacterial/fungal infections.

questions.stem: These paired fields show necrotizing granulomas with multinucleated giant cells and inflamed vessels in a patient with respiratory tract disease and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. Which serologic marker is most associated with this disease?

explanation.summary: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis features necrotizing granulomas plus vasculitis of the respiratory tract and kidney and is mediated by anti-proteinase 3 antibodies (PR3-ANCA, formerly c-ANCA).
explanation.whyCorrect: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis features necrotizing granulomas plus vasculitis of the respiratory tract and kidney and is mediated by anti-proteinase 3 antibodies (PR3-ANCA, formerly c-ANCA).

questions.stem: This glomerulus shows a proliferation of cells filling Bowman space and compressing the tuft. What is this histologic lesion called?

explanation.summary: Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis is defined by crescents of proliferating cells within Bowman space; it is a severe consequence of granulomatosis with polyangiitis rather than a granuloma itself.
explanation.whyCorrect: Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis is defined by crescents of proliferating cells within Bowman space; it is a severe consequence of granulomatosis with polyangiitis rather than a granuloma itself.

questions.stem: This skin cross-section shows a white, oval object embedded in the dermis surrounded by histiocytes and multinucleated giant cells. What type of reaction is this?

explanation.summary: A splinter (or other exogenous material) too large to be phagocytosed evokes a foreign body granuloma, identified by histiocytes and multinucleated giant cells surrounding the material without a strong T-cell-driven immune response.
explanation.whyCorrect: A splinter (or other exogenous material) too large to be phagocytosed evokes a foreign body granuloma, identified by histiocytes and multinucleated giant cells surrounding the material without a strong T-cell-driven immune response.

questions.stem: The left panel shows jagged material surrounded by epithelioid and giant cells; the right panel shows the same material glowing brightly under polarized light. What is this material and its relevance?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Birefringent talc from intravenous drug use

explanation.summary: Inhaled or injected drugs can introduce foreign material such as talc, which is birefringent (bright under polarized light) and provokes a foreign body granulomatous reaction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Inhaled or injected drugs can introduce foreign material such as talc, which is birefringent (bright under polarized light) and provokes a foreign body granulomatous reaction.

questions.stem: These fields show well-formed granulomas in a background of fibrosis with a star-shaped inclusion inside a giant cell. In a young adult with bilateral hilar adenopathy, what disease is most likely?

explanation.summary: Non-caseating granulomas with asteroid bodies (star-shaped inclusions) in a young adult are characteristic of sarcoidosis, a diagnosis of exclusion of unknown etiology.
explanation.whyCorrect: Non-caseating granulomas with asteroid bodies (star-shaped inclusions) in a young adult are characteristic of sarcoidosis, a diagnosis of exclusion of unknown etiology.

questions.stem: These images show tender, red, poorly demarcated nodules on the anterior lower legs during the acute phase and as they resolve. What is this nonspecific finding?

explanation.summary: Erythema nodosum presents as tender red nodules, usually on the anterior lower extremities; it is a nonspecific reactive panniculitis that can accompany sarcoidosis and other conditions.
explanation.whyCorrect: Erythema nodosum presents as tender red nodules, usually on the anterior lower extremities; it is a nonspecific reactive panniculitis that can accompany sarcoidosis and other conditions.

questions.stem: This colon shows a thick tan-white membrane on endoscopy, with histology revealing 'volcano-like' eruptions of neutrophils and debris from the crypts. Which organism is most likely responsible?

explanation.summary: A pseudomembrane of inflammatory cells and debris erupting from colonic crypts ('volcano lesions') is characteristic of C. difficile colitis, typically after antibiotic disruption of normal flora; its toxins A and B damage the mucosa.
explanation.whyCorrect: A pseudomembrane of inflammatory cells and debris erupting from colonic crypts ('volcano lesions') is characteristic of C. difficile colitis, typically after antibiotic disruption of normal flora; its toxins A and B damage the mucosa.

questions.stem: These heart valves show friable vegetations along the leaflet closure lines. What is the most likely underlying process producing these lesions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sterile fibrin deposition from a hypercoagulable state

explanation.summary: Friable vegetations on the valve leaflets are the hallmark of infective endocarditis, in which bacteria (e.g., S. viridans on the mitral valve, S. aureus on a congenitally bicuspid valve) seed and proliferate on the valve surface.
explanation.whyCorrect: Friable vegetations on the valve leaflets are the hallmark of infective endocarditis, in which bacteria (e.g., S. viridans on the mitral valve, S. aureus on a congenitally bicuspid valve) seed and proliferate on the valve surface.

questions.stem: This pharynx shows a thick gray membrane coating the tonsils and throat that bleeds when scraped away. The toxin of which organism produces this finding?

explanation.summary: Diphtheria toxin from C. diphtheriae produces a tightly adherent gray pseudomembrane of fibrin, dead cells, and inflammatory debris in the pharynx that can obstruct the airway.
explanation.whyCorrect: Diphtheria toxin from C. diphtheriae produces a tightly adherent gray pseudomembrane of fibrin, dead cells, and inflammatory debris in the pharynx that can obstruct the airway.

questions.stem: This clinical photograph shows grouped vesicles and crusted erosions on the vermilion border of the lip. Which agent is the most likely cause of these recurrent lesions?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Herpes simplex virus reactivating from a latent state

choices.label: D
choices.text: Treponema pallidum producing a primary chancre

explanation.summary: Herpes labialis (cold sores) results from reactivation of latent herpes simplex virus, producing grouped vesicles that crust on the lip margin. Latency in sensory ganglia and periodic reactivation are hallmark features.
explanation.whyCorrect: Herpes labialis (cold sores) results from reactivation of latent herpes simplex virus, producing grouped vesicles that crust on the lip margin. Latency in sensory ganglia and periodic reactivation are hallmark features.

questions.stem: This gross lung specimen shows the upper apices with softening and cavity formation. In tuberculosis, this apical cavitary pattern is most characteristic of which form of disease?

explanation.summary: A robust immune response in a previously sensitized host causes apical cavitation in the well-oxygenated upper lobes, the hallmark of secondary/reactivation pulmonary tuberculosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: A robust immune response in a previously sensitized host causes apical cavitation in the well-oxygenated upper lobes, the hallmark of secondary/reactivation pulmonary tuberculosis.

questions.stem: The organ on the right is studded with countless tiny granulomatous nodules resembling the millet seeds shown on the left. Which route of spread produces this diffuse seeding pattern?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lymphatic spread to a single node

explanation.summary: Miliary tuberculosis arises from hematogenous dissemination, seeding many organs with innumerable small granulomas that resemble millet seeds.
explanation.whyCorrect: Miliary tuberculosis arises from hematogenous dissemination, seeding many organs with innumerable small granulomas that resemble millet seeds.

questions.stem: This solitary, sharply demarcated ulcer with an indurated clean base appeared painlessly at the inoculation site about three weeks after infection. What is this lesion?

explanation.summary: A single painless ulcer with an indurated clean base at the inoculation site ~3 weeks after infection is the chancre of primary syphilis; chancroid, by contrast, is painful.
explanation.whyCorrect: A single painless ulcer with an indurated clean base at the inoculation site ~3 weeks after infection is the chancre of primary syphilis; chancroid, by contrast, is painful.

questions.stem: This skin shows scaly, papulosquamous plaques in a patient with syphilis. In which stage do these scaling lesions characteristically appear?

explanation.summary: Papulosquamous (scaling) rashes are a manifestation of secondary syphilis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Papulosquamous (scaling) rashes are a manifestation of secondary syphilis.

questions.stem: These images show annular (ring-like) plaques of the face and patches within the oral cavity in secondary syphilis. What is the chief clinical significance of these mucocutaneous lesions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They indicate irreversible tertiary organ damage

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are pathognomonic of HIV coinfection

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are highly infectious, teeming with spirochetes

choices.label: D
choices.text: They represent an allergic drug eruption

explanation.summary: Mucous patches and mucocutaneous lesions of secondary syphilis contain abundant spirochetes and are highly contagious.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mucous patches and mucocutaneous lesions of secondary syphilis contain abundant spirochetes and are highly contagious.

questions.stem: Compared with the normal cord on the left, the special-stained spinal cord on the right shows loss of axons and myelin confined to one region. Which part of the cord degenerates in this form of neurosyphilis?

explanation.summary: Tabes dorsalis, a form of neurosyphilis, degenerates the dorsal columns and dorsal roots, producing sensory ataxia and impaired proprioception.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tabes dorsalis, a form of neurosyphilis, degenerates the dorsal columns and dorsal roots, producing sensory ataxia and impaired proprioception.

questions.stem: These images from a neonate show a maculopapular rash of the feet and perianal moist plaques, appearing within months of birth. These are early manifestations of what condition?

explanation.summary: Transplacental Treponema pallidum causes congenital syphilis; early findings include snuffles, mucocutaneous lesions/condylomata lata, and rash.
explanation.whyCorrect: Transplacental Treponema pallidum causes congenital syphilis; early findings include snuffles, mucocutaneous lesions/condylomata lata, and rash.

questions.stem: This child shows small, notched, peg-shaped upper incisors together with a depressed nasal bridge. The dental abnormality is a hallmark of late congenital syphilis and carries which eponym?

explanation.summary: Notched, peg-shaped incisors (Hutchinson teeth) with saddle nose are late manifestations of congenital syphilis, part of the Hutchinson triad.
explanation.whyCorrect: Notched, peg-shaped incisors (Hutchinson teeth) with saddle nose are late manifestations of congenital syphilis, part of the Hutchinson triad.

questions.stem: This expanding annular skin lesion with a pale central clearing is the classic early manifestation of infection by which organism?

explanation.summary: The bullseye-like annular erythema with central clearing is erythema migrans, the hallmark of early localized Lyme disease caused by the spirochete Borrelia burgdorferi. It typically appears at the tick bite site and resolves over 4-12 weeks.
explanation.whyCorrect: The bullseye-like annular erythema with central clearing is erythema migrans, the hallmark of early localized Lyme disease caused by the spirochete Borrelia burgdorferi. It typically appears at the tick bite site and resolves over 4-12 weeks.

questions.stem: These white, curd-like plaques on the oral mucosa of a patient with advancing HIV infection are caused by which organism?

explanation.summary: Oral candidiasis (thrush) presents as white patches that can be scraped off, reflecting superficial fungal overgrowth of Candida when cell-mediated immunity declines. It is a common early clue to immunosuppression in chronic HIV infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Oral candidiasis (thrush) presents as white patches that can be scraped off, reflecting superficial fungal overgrowth of Candida when cell-mediated immunity declines. It is a common early clue to immunosuppression in chronic HIV infection.

questions.stem: This severe, deep perirectal ulceration in an AIDS patient is most characteristically caused by reactivation of which organism?

explanation.summary: In advanced immunosuppression, HSV can reactivate to produce chronic, severe perirectal (and oral/esophageal) ulcerations that fail to heal, in contrast to the small self-limited lesions seen in immunocompetent hosts.
explanation.whyCorrect: In advanced immunosuppression, HSV can reactivate to produce chronic, severe perirectal (and oral/esophageal) ulcerations that fail to heal, in contrast to the small self-limited lesions seen in immunocompetent hosts.

questions.stem: The upper donor liver (A) appears pale, yellow, and greasy compared with the deeper red-brown normal liver below (B). This gross change is caused by accumulation of what substance within hepatocytes?

explanation.summary: The pale, yellowed, enlarged appearance is characteristic of hepatic steatosis, in which triglyceride accumulates in hepatocytes. Significant macrovesicular steatosis is a relative or absolute contraindication to using the liver for transplant.
explanation.whyCorrect: The pale, yellowed, enlarged appearance is characteristic of hepatic steatosis, in which triglyceride accumulates in hepatocytes. Significant macrovesicular steatosis is a relative or absolute contraindication to using the liver for transplant.

questions.stem: This H&E-stained donor liver shows numerous large, clear, sharply defined vacuoles that displace hepatocyte nuclei to the cell periphery. What is this finding?

explanation.summary: A single large fat droplet pushing the nucleus to the edge of the hepatocyte defines macrovesicular steatosis. Confirmation of greater than 30 percent macrovesicular steatosis is associated with poor early graft function.
explanation.whyCorrect: A single large fat droplet pushing the nucleus to the edge of the hepatocyte defines macrovesicular steatosis. Confirmation of greater than 30 percent macrovesicular steatosis is associated with poor early graft function.

questions.stem: In the right-hand panel, a special stain highlights blue-staining bands that bridge between adjacent regions of the liver parenchyma. These bands represent excess deposition of what?

explanation.summary: The trichrome stain colors collagen blue, revealing bridging fibrosis. Fibrosis is graded by extent of involvement and is an important determinant of donor organ viability.
explanation.whyCorrect: The trichrome stain colors collagen blue, revealing bridging fibrosis. Fibrosis is graded by extent of involvement and is an important determinant of donor organ viability.

questions.stem: The arrow points to hepatocytes that are shrunken, deeply eosinophilic, and have lost their nuclei while retaining cellular outlines. What process does this represent?

explanation.summary: Loss of nuclear staining with preserved cell outline is coagulative necrosis of hepatocytes. Greater than 10 percent necrosis may disqualify a donor organ from transplantation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Loss of nuclear staining with preserved cell outline is coagulative necrosis of hepatocytes. Greater than 10 percent necrosis may disqualify a donor organ from transplantation.

questions.stem: This frozen section of a donor kidney shows arrows indicating expansion of the space between tubules by scarring together with a mononuclear cell infiltrate. What two chronic changes are demonstrated?

explanation.summary: Widening of the interstitium by collagen (fibrosis) accompanied by inflammatory cells constitutes interstitial fibrosis and inflammation, a key pathology assessed in donor kidney biopsies.
explanation.whyCorrect: Widening of the interstitium by collagen (fibrosis) accompanied by inflammatory cells constitutes interstitial fibrosis and inflammation, a key pathology assessed in donor kidney biopsies.

questions.stem: Across these three arterial cross-sections the lumen becomes progressively narrowed. The wall change responsible is thickening of which layer of the vessel?

explanation.summary: Arterial sclerosis in the kidney reflects deposition of collagenous matrix and smooth muscle between the endothelium and internal elastic lamina, thickening the intima and narrowing the lumen.
explanation.whyCorrect: Arterial sclerosis in the kidney reflects deposition of collagenous matrix and smooth muscle between the endothelium and internal elastic lamina, thickening the intima and narrowing the lumen.

questions.stem: The wall of this small vessel is thickened by glassy, homogeneous, deeply eosinophilic material with loss of normal wall structure. What is this change called?

explanation.summary: Deposition of homogeneous eosinophilic protein material in the arteriolar wall is arteriolar hyalinization (hyaline arteriolosclerosis), commonly associated with hypertension and diabetes in donor kidneys.
explanation.whyCorrect: Deposition of homogeneous eosinophilic protein material in the arteriolar wall is arteriolar hyalinization (hyaline arteriolosclerosis), commonly associated with hypertension and diabetes in donor kidneys.

questions.stem: Compared with the well-organized capillary tuft on the left, the glomerulus on the right (arrows) has lost its capillary architecture. It has been replaced by what?

explanation.summary: Global glomerulosclerosis is scarring and hyalinization affecting more than half of the glomerulus, obliterating the capillary loops with acellular hyaline material. The proportion of sclerosed glomeruli helps determine kidney viability.
explanation.whyCorrect: Global glomerulosclerosis is scarring and hyalinization affecting more than half of the glomerulus, obliterating the capillary loops with acellular hyaline material. The proportion of sclerosed glomeruli helps determine kidney viability.

questions.stem: The paired schematic and kidney histology shown depict antibody binding to graft endothelium triggering complement activation, endothelial damage, and platelet-fibrin thrombi with severe ischemic injury. Which type of transplant rejection is illustrated?

explanation.summary: Preformed recipient antibodies against ABO or MHC antigens on graft endothelium activate complement within minutes to hours, causing thrombosis, ischemia, and necrosis (a type II hypersensitivity reaction). The glomerular fibrin thrombi shown are the histologic hallmark of hyperacute rejection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Preformed recipient antibodies against ABO or MHC antigens on graft endothelium activate complement within minutes to hours, causing thrombosis, ischemia, and necrosis (a type II hypersensitivity reaction). The glomerular fibrin thrombi shown are the histologic hallmark of hyperacute rejection.

questions.stem: This liver biopsy shows a portal tract markedly expanded by lymphocytes and plasma cells surrounding a destructive granulomatous reaction centered on an interlobular bile duct. This 'florid duct lesion' is characteristic of which disease?

explanation.summary: The florid duct lesion, a granulomatous destruction of interlobular bile ducts with surrounding lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate, is the hallmark histologic finding of primary biliary cholangitis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The florid duct lesion, a granulomatous destruction of interlobular bile ducts with surrounding lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate, is the hallmark histologic finding of primary biliary cholangitis.

questions.stem: This portal tract shows a bile duct entrapped in dense concentric 'onion-skin' periductal fibrosis. Which condition is indicated by this classic lesion?

explanation.summary: Concentric 'onion-skin' periductal fibrosis progressing to obliteration of the bile duct is the characteristic histology of primary sclerosing cholangitis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Concentric 'onion-skin' periductal fibrosis progressing to obliteration of the bile duct is the characteristic histology of primary sclerosing cholangitis.

questions.stem: The left panel shows normal hyaline cartilage for comparison, and the right panel shows a de-differentiated malignant tumor. From which normal cell type is this malignant tumor derived?

explanation.summary: This is a de-differentiated chondrosarcoma; despite anaplasia, the retained cartilage-like component identifies chondrocytes as the cell of origin.
explanation.whyCorrect: This is a de-differentiated chondrosarcoma; despite anaplasia, the retained cartilage-like component identifies chondrocytes as the cell of origin.

questions.stem: This esophageal biopsy shows normal stratified squamous epithelium adjacent to a region where the lining has been replaced by simple columnar (glandular) epithelium. This metaplastic condition is known as what?

explanation.summary: Chronic acid reflux damages esophageal squamous epithelium, which is replaced by protective columnar epithelium with goblet cells; this glandular metaplasia defines Barrett esophagus and carries a risk of adenocarcinoma.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic acid reflux damages esophageal squamous epithelium, which is replaced by protective columnar epithelium with goblet cells; this glandular metaplasia defines Barrett esophagus and carries a risk of adenocarcinoma.

questions.stem: These paired breast sections show tumor cells infiltrating in a single-file pattern (left, H&E) and absence of the membranous brown immunostain seen normally (right). The loss of the marker highlighted here most directly impairs which cellular function?

explanation.summary: The single-file infiltration and loss of membranous staining reflect loss of E-cadherin, a homophilic adhesion molecule; its loss disrupts cell-to-cell adhesion, a hallmark of invasive lobular carcinoma. The other functions are unrelated to E-cadherin.
explanation.whyCorrect: The single-file infiltration and loss of membranous staining reflect loss of E-cadherin, a homophilic adhesion molecule; its loss disrupts cell-to-cell adhesion, a hallmark of invasive lobular carcinoma. The other functions are unrelated to E-cadherin.

questions.stem: This uterine sarcoma shows a rounded nest of neoplastic cells filling and expanding a vascular space. A tumor demonstrating this pattern of spread would be expected to metastasize primarily by which route?

explanation.summary: Sarcomas of mesenchymal origin invade vessels and characteristically metastasize hematogenously, as shown by tumor filling the vascular lumen. Lymphatic spread is typical of carcinomas, transcoelomic seeding occurs across body cavities, and perineural/direct extension are different routes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sarcomas of mesenchymal origin invade vessels and characteristically metastasize hematogenously, as shown by tumor filling the vascular lumen. Lymphatic spread is typical of carcinomas, transcoelomic seeding occurs across body cavities, and perineural/direct extension are different routes.

questions.stem: This gross specimen shows the omentum diffusely studded and thickened by nodular tumor deposits. By which mechanism of spread did the ovarian carcinoma most likely reach this site?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Transcoelomic (seeding) spread across the peritoneal cavity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hematogenous spread through the portal vein

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct invasion through the uterine wall

explanation.summary: Ovarian carcinoma classically seeds the peritoneal cavity, coating the omentum with tumor (omental caking) via transcoelomic spread. The other routes do not explain diffuse serosal/omental studding.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ovarian carcinoma classically seeds the peritoneal cavity, coating the omentum with tumor (omental caking) via transcoelomic spread. The other routes do not explain diffuse serosal/omental studding.

questions.stem: This graph plots the prevalence of RAS gene family mutations across many human cancer types. In which malignancy are RAS-family (KRAS) mutations found at among the highest frequencies?

explanation.summary: KRAS mutations are extremely common in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, one of the highest-prevalence tumor types shown; the graph illustrates the variability of HRAS, KRAS, and NRAS mutation frequencies across cancers.
explanation.whyCorrect: KRAS mutations are extremely common in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, one of the highest-prevalence tumor types shown; the graph illustrates the variability of HRAS, KRAS, and NRAS mutation frequencies across cancers.

questions.stem: This histologic field shows malignant cells forming irregular duct-like glandular spaces in the pancreas. This KRAS-driven, smoking-associated, nearly always fatal neoplasm is best identified as which entity?

explanation.summary: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma forms infiltrative duct-like glandular structures composed of neoplastic cells, commonly harbors KRAS mutations, and carries a very poor prognosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma forms infiltrative duct-like glandular structures composed of neoplastic cells, commonly harbors KRAS mutations, and carries a very poor prognosis.

questions.stem: The left panel shows a brown, irregular skin lesion and the right panel a neoplasm containing brown pigment. This pigment is a clue to the transformed cell of origin. What is this neoplasm, commonly driven by activating BRAF mutations?

explanation.summary: Cutaneous melanoma arises from transformed melanocytes, which produce brown melanin pigment visible histologically; activating BRAF (V600E) mutations are a common oncogenic driver and therapeutic target.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cutaneous melanoma arises from transformed melanocytes, which produce brown melanin pigment visible histologically; activating BRAF (V600E) mutations are a common oncogenic driver and therapeutic target.

questions.stem: This hypercellular bone marrow shows increased granulocytes with eosinophils and small, hypolobated megakaryocytes. These findings are characteristic of which myeloproliferative neoplasm driven by a BCR::ABL fusion?

explanation.summary: CML marrow is hypercellular with granulocytic hyperplasia, eosinophilia, and small hypolobated megakaryocytes; it is driven by the BCR::ABL fusion producing constitutive tyrosine kinase activity.
explanation.whyCorrect: CML marrow is hypercellular with granulocytic hyperplasia, eosinophilia, and small hypolobated megakaryocytes; it is driven by the BCR::ABL fusion producing constitutive tyrosine kinase activity.

questions.stem: These paired histologic fields compare a benign polypoid glandular proliferation (left) with a malignant lesion (right). What feature defines the lesion on the right as adenocarcinoma rather than adenoma?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deposition of osteoid by the tumor cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: A polypoid growth confined above the muscularis mucosae

choices.label: C
choices.text: Restored nuclear polarity and absent atypia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Invasion of neoplastic epithelium through the basement membrane into the stroma

choices.label: E
choices.text: Formation of true rosettes with central lumina

explanation.summary: Colorectal adenomas are non-invasive polypoid growths, whereas adenocarcinoma is defined by invasion of the neoplastic epithelium beyond the basement membrane into the lamina propria and deeper tissue.
explanation.whyCorrect: Colorectal adenomas are non-invasive polypoid growths, whereas adenocarcinoma is defined by invasion of the neoplastic epithelium beyond the basement membrane into the lamina propria and deeper tissue.

questions.stem: A patient exhibits the flat, evenly pigmented skin patches (left) and the soft cutaneous tumors of peripheral nerve (right) pictured here. These findings are diagnostic of which condition?

explanation.summary: Café-au-lait macules and multiple cutaneous neurofibromas are hallmark manifestations of neurofibromatosis type 1, caused by loss of the NF1 GTPase-activating protein on chromosome 17.
explanation.whyCorrect: Café-au-lait macules and multiple cutaneous neurofibromas are hallmark manifestations of neurofibromatosis type 1, caused by loss of the NF1 GTPase-activating protein on chromosome 17.

questions.stem: This wheel arranges eight acquired capabilities—such as evading growth suppressors, resisting cell death, and inducing angiogenesis—around a central tumor. What do these eight traits collectively represent?

choices.label: C
choices.text: The phases of the cell cycle

choices.label: E
choices.text: The components of the WNT pathway

explanation.summary: The figure depicts the eight fundamental hallmarks of cancer—the capabilities neoplasms acquire to sustain growth, evade suppression and immune destruction, and invade and metastasize.
explanation.whyCorrect: The figure depicts the eight fundamental hallmarks of cancer—the capabilities neoplasms acquire to sustain growth, evade suppression and immune destruction, and invade and metastasize.

questions.stem: These injured kidney tubule cells show swelling and increased pink cytoplasm. The fading, washed-out nuclei in some cells reflect which pattern of nuclear change?

explanation.summary: Karyolysis is the fading of basophilic nuclear staining as chromatin is degraded. It is distinct from pyknosis (shrunken dark nuclei) and karyorrhexis (nuclear fragmentation), accompanying the eosinophilia and swelling of injured cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Karyolysis is the fading of basophilic nuclear staining as chromatin is degraded. It is distinct from pyknosis (shrunken dark nuclei) and karyorrhexis (nuclear fragmentation), accompanying the eosinophilia and swelling of injured cells.

questions.stem: This liver from a patient who cannot properly excrete bile shows a yellow-brown pigment within cells. What is this pigment?

explanation.summary: Bilirubin is a yellow-brown pigment from heme breakdown that accumulates when bile excretion is impaired. It is distinct from hemosiderin, the iron-containing blood breakdown product.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bilirubin is a yellow-brown pigment from heme breakdown that accumulates when bile excretion is impaired. It is distinct from hemosiderin, the iron-containing blood breakdown product.

questions.stem: This gross specimen shows hard, chalky white deposits on the cusps of a damaged, atherosclerotic aortic valve. Given normal systemic calcium levels, what type of calcification is this?

explanation.summary: Dystrophic calcification is calcium deposition in damaged or dying tissue under normal systemic calcium homeostasis, as in this atherosclerotic aortic valve. Metastatic calcification instead occurs in normal tissue with hypercalcemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dystrophic calcification is calcium deposition in damaged or dying tissue under normal systemic calcium homeostasis, as in this atherosclerotic aortic valve. Metastatic calcification instead occurs in normal tissue with hypercalcemia.

questions.stem: This bisected uterus shows a bulky, thickened endometrial lining compared with the normal region. With excess estrogen stimulation, what adaptive change does the bulky area represent?

explanation.summary: Excess estrogen drives increased proliferation of endometrial glandular cells (hyperplasia), producing a bulky lining and a cause of abnormal uterine bleeding.
explanation.whyCorrect: Excess estrogen drives increased proliferation of endometrial glandular cells (hyperplasia), producing a bulky lining and a cause of abnormal uterine bleeding.

questions.stem: This patient shows extreme photosensitivity with dense freckling and lesions concentrated on sun-exposed skin of the face and neck. An inherited defect in which DNA repair pathway underlies this presentation?

explanation.summary: Xeroderma pigmentosum results from loss-of-function mutations in nucleotide excision repair, leaving patients unable to remove UV-induced pyrimidine dimers, producing severe photosensitivity, freckling in sun-exposed areas, and greatly increased skin cancer risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Xeroderma pigmentosum results from loss-of-function mutations in nucleotide excision repair, leaving patients unable to remove UV-induced pyrimidine dimers, producing severe photosensitivity, freckling in sun-exposed areas, and greatly increased skin cancer risk.

questions.stem: This schematic shows the MSH2-MSH6 complex identifying an error in newly synthesized DNA and the MLH1-PMS2 complex excising the incorrect base before resynthesis. Germline loss of the pathway shown predisposes to which hereditary cancer syndrome?

explanation.summary: The figure depicts mismatch repair. Germline mutation of a mismatch-repair gene (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) causes Lynch syndrome (hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer), producing microsatellite instability and markedly increased colorectal and endometrial cancer risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: The figure depicts mismatch repair. Germline mutation of a mismatch-repair gene (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) causes Lynch syndrome (hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer), producing microsatellite instability and markedly increased colorectal and endometrial cancer risk.

questions.stem: These images show dilated tortuous blood vessels in the conjunctiva of the eye (A) and on the skin (B, C) of the same patient, who developed ataxia, chorea, and neuropathy before age five. A defect in which DNA repair mechanism causes this disorder?

explanation.summary: The dilated vessels (telangiectasia) with early-onset ataxia indicate ataxia telangiectasia, caused by defective nonhomologous end joining of double-strand breaks. The same defect impairs V(D)J recombination, producing immunodeficiency, and predisposes to leukemia and lymphoma.
explanation.whyCorrect: The dilated vessels (telangiectasia) with early-onset ataxia indicate ataxia telangiectasia, caused by defective nonhomologous end joining of double-strand breaks. The same defect impairs V(D)J recombination, producing immunodeficiency, and predisposes to leukemia and lymphoma.

questions.stem: These lesions, including a pedunculated example and one with a velvety surface, are removed during colonoscopy. Left in place, they are the principal precursor to which malignancy?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Squamous cell carcinoma of the anus

explanation.summary: The images show colonic adenomas (adenomatous polyps). Through the adenoma-carcinoma sequence, crowded neoplastic glands progressively accumulate mutations and can invade beyond the basement membrane into the lamina propria, giving rise to colorectal adenocarcinoma. Screening colonoscopy allows their removal before malignant transformation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The images show colonic adenomas (adenomatous polyps). Through the adenoma-carcinoma sequence, crowded neoplastic glands progressively accumulate mutations and can invade beyond the basement membrane into the lamina propria, giving rise to colorectal adenocarcinoma. Screening colonoscopy allows their removal before malignant transformation.

questions.stem: This illustration depicts a child from an equatorial African region with a rapidly enlarging mass distorting the mandible. This presentation is classic for which neoplasm?

explanation.summary: Endemic Burkitt lymphoma is virtually always EBV-positive and classically presents in African children as a large, rapidly growing mandibular/jaw mass.
explanation.whyCorrect: Endemic Burkitt lymphoma is virtually always EBV-positive and classically presents in African children as a large, rapidly growing mandibular/jaw mass.

questions.stem: This gross cross-section of liver shows a large hemorrhagic mass arising within a background of chronic liver injury. Chronic infection with which virus is the most common global cause of this tumor?

explanation.summary: Hepatocellular carcinoma most often arises from longstanding inflammation-mediated cellular damage and cirrhosis; hepatitis B virus is the leading worldwide cause.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hepatocellular carcinoma most often arises from longstanding inflammation-mediated cellular damage and cirrhosis; hepatitis B virus is the leading worldwide cause.

questions.stem: The left image shows a raised, rough papule on a child's finger and the right shows its histology with a hyperkeratotic squamous proliferation. Which HPV serotypes typically cause these benign lesions?

explanation.summary: Verruca vulgaris (common warts) are benign hyperkeratotic squamous papillomas caused by low-risk HPV serotypes 1, 2, 4, and 7.
explanation.whyCorrect: Verruca vulgaris (common warts) are benign hyperkeratotic squamous papillomas caused by low-risk HPV serotypes 1, 2, 4, and 7.

questions.stem: This bladder biopsy shows parasitic eggs bearing a terminal spine surrounded by chronic inflammation. Long-term infection with this parasite predisposes to which malignancy?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Squamous cell carcinoma of the bladder

choices.label: E
choices.text: Small cell carcinoma of the bladder

explanation.summary: Schistosoma haematobium eggs (terminal spine) cause chronic bladder inflammation and metaplasia, predisposing to squamous cell carcinoma of the bladder.
explanation.whyCorrect: Schistosoma haematobium eggs (terminal spine) cause chronic bladder inflammation and metaplasia, predisposing to squamous cell carcinoma of the bladder.

questions.stem: This schematic shows a viable cell kept alive by the anti-apoptotic protein BCL2 at the mitochondrion. Pathologic overexpression of this protein, via a t(14;18) translocation, characteristically drives which malignancy?

explanation.summary: BCL2 keeps the outer mitochondrial membrane impermeable, blocking cytochrome c release and preventing apoptosis. The t(14;18) translocation places BCL2 under an immunoglobulin promoter, causing overexpression that resists apoptosis and produces follicular lymphoma.
explanation.whyCorrect: BCL2 keeps the outer mitochondrial membrane impermeable, blocking cytochrome c release and preventing apoptosis. The t(14;18) translocation places BCL2 under an immunoglobulin promoter, causing overexpression that resists apoptosis and produces follicular lymphoma.

questions.stem: The left gross panel shows a pale yellow wedge-shaped lesion and the right microscopic panel shows preserved tissue outlines with loss of nuclei. This pattern of necrosis is most typical of which underlying cause?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ischemia (loss of blood supply) outside the CNS

explanation.summary: Coagulative necrosis preserves the tissue architecture (ghost outlines) while cells die, and is the hallmark of ischemic infarction in solid organs other than the brain. The wedge-shaped pale infarct and retained cell outlines are classic.
explanation.whyCorrect: Coagulative necrosis preserves the tissue architecture (ghost outlines) while cells die, and is the hallmark of ischemic infarction in solid organs other than the brain. The wedge-shaped pale infarct and retained cell outlines are classic.

questions.stem: This gross brain specimen shows necrotic tissue that has been converted into a soft, semi-liquid, cavitated mass. What mechanism accounts for this liquefied consistency?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enzymatic (hydrolytic) digestion of the dead tissue

explanation.summary: Liquefactive necrosis results when hydrolytic enzymes digest dead tissue into a viscous liquid. It is the characteristic pattern of central nervous system infarcts and of abscesses. Protein denaturation instead yields coagulative necrosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Liquefactive necrosis results when hydrolytic enzymes digest dead tissue into a viscous liquid. It is the characteristic pattern of central nervous system infarcts and of abscesses. Protein denaturation instead yields coagulative necrosis.

questions.stem: This clinical photograph shows blackened, mummified digits. This descriptive term for necrosis is most commonly applied in the setting of what?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ischemia of a limb or its digits

explanation.summary: Gangrenous necrosis is a clinical term for necrosis of a limb or digits, typically from loss of blood supply. Dry gangrene is largely coagulative; wet gangrene adds superimposed liquefactive necrosis from bacterial infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Gangrenous necrosis is a clinical term for necrosis of a limb or digits, typically from loss of blood supply. Dry gangrene is largely coagulative; wet gangrene adds superimposed liquefactive necrosis from bacterial infection.

questions.stem: This gross specimen shows a soft, friable, cheese-like area of necrosis. This pattern is classically associated with infection by which organism?

explanation.summary: Caseous necrosis has a friable, cheese-like gross appearance and is classically caused by tuberculosis. Microscopically it shows granulomas with fragmented, granular debris, distinguishing it from other necrosis patterns.
explanation.whyCorrect: Caseous necrosis has a friable, cheese-like gross appearance and is classically caused by tuberculosis. Microscopically it shows granulomas with fragmented, granular debris, distinguishing it from other necrosis patterns.

questions.stem: This gross specimen shows adipose tissue studded with chalky-white deposits. These deposits form through saponification involving calcium and what other substance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Free fatty acids released from necrotic fat

explanation.summary: In fat necrosis, released lipases (e.g., in acute pancreatitis) break down adipose tissue; the liberated free fatty acids combine with calcium in a saponification reaction to form the chalky-white deposits seen here.
explanation.whyCorrect: In fat necrosis, released lipases (e.g., in acute pancreatitis) break down adipose tissue; the liberated free fatty acids combine with calcium in a saponification reaction to form the chalky-white deposits seen here.

questions.stem: This histology field shows a blood vessel wall with a bright pink, smudgy deposit and a surrounding inflammatory infiltrate. This pattern of necrosis is most characteristically seen in which condition?

explanation.summary: Fibrinoid necrosis occurs when immune complexes and plasma proteins (fibrin) deposit in vessel walls, producing a bright pink, smudgy appearance with inflammation. It is characteristic of autoimmune small-vessel vasculitides.
explanation.whyCorrect: Fibrinoid necrosis occurs when immune complexes and plasma proteins (fibrin) deposit in vessel walls, producing a bright pink, smudgy appearance with inflammation. It is characteristic of autoimmune small-vessel vasculitides.

questions.stem: The enlarged, overlapping nuclei with clearing shown in this thyroid lesion are diagnostic features of which malignancy?

explanation.summary: Papillary thyroid carcinoma is characterized by nuclear enlargement, nuclear overlap, and optically clear (Orphan Annie eye) nuclei with grooves.
explanation.whyCorrect: Papillary thyroid carcinoma is characterized by nuclear enlargement, nuclear overlap, and optically clear (Orphan Annie eye) nuclei with grooves.

questions.stem: This skin section shows the epidermis lifted away from the underlying dermis by a collection of pale, watery fluid. Which morphologic pattern of inflammation is depicted?

explanation.summary: A blister formed by accumulation of protein-poor, cell-poor (transudate-like) fluid separating the epidermis represents serous inflammation.
explanation.whyCorrect: A blister formed by accumulation of protein-poor, cell-poor (transudate-like) fluid separating the epidermis represents serous inflammation.

questions.stem: Panel A shows a shaggy, roughened surface coating this heart and panel B shows a pink meshwork overlying the pericardial surface. Which type of inflammatory exudate is shown?

explanation.summary: Increased vascular permeability allows fibrinogen to leak and deposit as fibrin on the pericardial surface, producing a fibrinous exudate (fibrinous pericarditis).
explanation.whyCorrect: Increased vascular permeability allows fibrinogen to leak and deposit as fibrin on the pericardial surface, producing a fibrinous exudate (fibrinous pericarditis).

questions.stem: This gross specimen (A) and low-power cross-section (B) show a punched-out defect penetrating through the mucosal surface with an inflammatory exudate at its base. Which morphologic pattern of inflammation is shown?

explanation.summary: An ulcer is a local defect of an epithelial surface produced by sloughing of inflamed necrotic tissue, here a chronic duodenal ulcer.
explanation.whyCorrect: An ulcer is a local defect of an epithelial surface produced by sloughing of inflamed necrotic tissue, here a chronic duodenal ulcer.

questions.stem: This lung histology shows a granuloma with a central zone of caseous necrosis rimmed by epithelioid macrophages and multinucleated giant cells. Infection by which organism most classically produces this pattern?

explanation.summary: Caseating (necrotizing) granulomas in the lung are the hallmark of Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection. The listed pyogenic bacteria produce acute suppurative inflammation with abscesses rather than organized granulomas with central caseous necrosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Caseating (necrotizing) granulomas in the lung are the hallmark of Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection. The listed pyogenic bacteria produce acute suppurative inflammation with abscesses rather than organized granulomas with central caseous necrosis.

questions.stem: This lung biopsy shows a well-formed granuloma composed of epithelioid histiocytes, lymphocytes, and macrophages, notably without any central necrosis. Which disease most characteristically produces this non-necrotizing pattern?

explanation.summary: Compact, non-caseating granulomas that lack central necrosis are classic for sarcoidosis. Tuberculosis and histoplasmosis usually caseate, cat-scratch disease shows suppurative granulomas, and while Crohn's granulomas are non-caseating they occur in the gut, not the lung.
explanation.whyCorrect: Compact, non-caseating granulomas that lack central necrosis are classic for sarcoidosis. Tuberculosis and histoplasmosis usually caseate, cat-scratch disease shows suppurative granulomas, and while Crohn's granulomas are non-caseating they occur in the gut, not the lung.

questions.stem: Compared with the granuloma of tuberculosis, the lesion depicted in this schematic of sarcoidosis characteristically lacks which central feature?

explanation.summary: Sarcoid granulomas are non-caseating, so they lack the central caseous necrosis typical of tuberculosis. They still contain multinucleated giant cells, epithelioid macrophages, lymphocytes, and a surrounding fibrous rim, so those are present rather than absent.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sarcoid granulomas are non-caseating, so they lack the central caseous necrosis typical of tuberculosis. They still contain multinucleated giant cells, epithelioid macrophages, lymphocytes, and a surrounding fibrous rim, so those are present rather than absent.

questions.stem: These stacked cross-sections illustrate repair of a large, gaping skin wound over successive time points. What mechanism is primarily responsible for closing the defect shown?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Complete regeneration of the epidermis without any scar

choices.label: B
choices.text: Surgical apposition of the wound edges with minimal scarring

choices.label: C
choices.text: Filling by granulation tissue followed by myofibroblast-mediated wound contraction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Repair by regeneration of permanent cardiac muscle

choices.label: E
choices.text: Resolution of the wound without any inflammatory phase

explanation.summary: Healing by secondary intention occurs with extensive tissue loss: the large defect fills with granulation tissue, and myofibroblasts form a contractile network that pulls the wound together, reducing the defect over weeks.
explanation.whyCorrect: Healing by secondary intention occurs with extensive tissue loss: the large defect fills with granulation tissue, and myofibroblasts form a contractile network that pulls the wound together, reducing the defect over weeks.

questions.stem: In the clinical image on the right, the raised scar extends beyond the boundaries of the original wound, and the accompanying histology shows thick, brightly eosinophilic collagen fibers arranged haphazardly. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: A keloid is exuberant scarring that extends beyond the boundaries of the original wound and shows thick, brightly eosinophilic collagen arranged haphazardly; unlike hypertrophic scars, which stay within the original wound and may regress, keloids do not regress on their own.
explanation.whyCorrect: A keloid is exuberant scarring that extends beyond the boundaries of the original wound and shows thick, brightly eosinophilic collagen arranged haphazardly; unlike hypertrophic scars, which stay within the original wound and may regress, keloids do not regress on their own.

questions.stem: The lower histologic panels of this chronic-wound montage show necrotic surface debris, inflammation, and underlying granulation tissue. Which histologic feature best defines the lesion as an ulcer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Neoplastic proliferation of squamous epithelial cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: An encapsulated collection of pus with intact overlying epithelium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Full-thickness regeneration of the epidermis with an intact surface

choices.label: D
choices.text: Loss of the overlying epithelial surface with underlying inflammation and granulation tissue

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hyperplasia of keratinocytes forming a raised nodule

explanation.summary: An ulcer is a wound lacking an intact epithelial surface; histologically it correlates with loss of the overlying epithelium plus surface necrotic debris, inflammation, and underlying granulation tissue, as seen in these venous, arterial, diabetic, and pressure-related chronic wounds.
explanation.whyCorrect: An ulcer is a wound lacking an intact epithelial surface; histologically it correlates with loss of the overlying epithelium plus surface necrotic debris, inflammation, and underlying granulation tissue, as seen in these venous, arterial, diabetic, and pressure-related chronic wounds.
